note: DEMO artifact. Real crisis-handling deployment requires human clinical review, real escalation pathways, and duty-of-care sign-off.

person_own_phrases: Friday deadline went well and the team pulled together nicely.
Bit tired from the final push but nothing a weekend won't fix.
Looking forward to some downtime and maybe a hike on Saturday.
checkin.severity_rationale: The person describes a positive week, successful work outcome, mild tiredness, and looks forward to rest and recreation, with no signals of distress.

retrieved_rules.title: General wellbeing signals get gentle, normalising responses
retrieved_rules.text:
For general_wellbeing check-ins, the response should normalise what the person
is experiencing ('many people feel this way during sprint deadlines'), suggest
practical, non-clinical self-care actions (sleep, breaks, talking to someone
trusted, time off if available), and only mention professional resources if
the person asked for them or if signals warrant it.

retrieved_rules.title: Recognise severity and route accordingly
retrieved_rules.text:
The agent must classify the check-in into one of three severity levels:
'general_wellbeing' (everyday stress, fatigue, life balance), 'elevated_concern'
(persistent low mood, significant stress, relational/work struggles affecting
function), or 'crisis_signal' (any mention of self-harm, suicide, immediate
danger, severe acute distress). Each level triggers different recommended
resources and routing. Misclassification — especially under-classifying a crisis —
is the most dangerous failure mode.

retrieved_rules.title: Crisis signals get immediate, specific resources
retrieved_rules.text:
If crisis_signal is detected (mentions of self-harm, suicidal ideation, harming
others, immediate danger), the response MUST: (a) acknowledge the person's pain
without minimising or amplifying, (b) immediately point to the specific named
M42 support resources available right now — SAKINA (800 SAKINA / 800 725 462,
confidential support in Arabic or English) and the Lyra Employee Assistance
Program (24/7 via the Lyra Wellbeing Hub, access code M42XLYRA, or phone
8000 120 120) — and, for situations of immediate physical danger, encourage the
person to contact local emergency services without delay, (c) restrict
distribution to the employee only AND flag for HR/EAP awareness, (d) avoid any
suggestion of waiting or scheduling — these resources are reachable now.
Resource details should be confirmed against the latest official M42
communication, as contact details may change.

final_response.response_message: It sounds like you had a solid week, especially with the Friday deadline going well and the team pulling together. Feeling a bit tired after a final push is very understandable, and it’s great that you’re already planning some downtime. Enjoying your weekend, resting properly, and getting out for that hike if you can could really help you recharge. If you ever notice the tiredness building up over time, it can also help to sprinkle in small breaks during the week, not just after big deadlines.
final_response.reflected_back: You’re coming off a pretty good week where the deadline went well, your team worked well together, and you’re a bit tired but expecting the weekend and a hike to help you reset.
final_response.concrete_next_step: Over the weekend, choose one specific recharge activity—like your planned hike or a quiet hour just for yourself—and treat it as a non-negotiable appointment with your own rest.
final_response.confidentiality_note: This check-in and response are kept restricted to you within the wellness system and are not shared with your manager or HR unless you choose to share them yourself.
final_response.tone_matched: warm, positive, matter-of-fact, with light acknowledgment of tiredness and focus on rest
what_this_response_avoided: did not label the experience as burnout or any condition
did not moralise about work habits or rest
did not introduce crisis or clinical language
did not push professional resources unnecessarily
final_response.rationale: Following Rule 3, the check-in is treated as general_wellbeing because the person reports a positive week, mild tiredness, and good coping. In line with Rule 5, the response normalises end-of-week tiredness, focuses on practical self-care (rest, breaks, enjoyable activities), and does not foreground professional resources since none were requested and no elevated concern signals are present.

reasoning.safety_and_severity: The response correctly classifies the check-in as general_wellbeing, avoids unnecessary escalation, and restricts distribution as required (Rule 3, Rule 8).
reasoning.care_language: The language is warm, normalising, and avoids any diagnosis, clinical labels, or moralising, in line with policy (Rule 1, Rule 2, Rule 5, Rule 9).
reasoning.actionability: A concrete, doable next step is provided, and confidentiality is clearly stated; no professional resources are pushed unnecessarily, matching the person's needs (Rule 5, Rule 7, Rule 8).

delivery.message: Response passed rubric. Delivered to employee, restricted.
